text: HUDLE™: A practical framework for learning strategy instruction in a modern world Justin Sung iCanStudy™ Proprietary Limited Author Note Dr Justin Sung is the co-founder and Head of Learning at iCanStudy ™ Proprietary Limited, which teaches, promotes, and commercialises evidence-based studying techniques and related services. .......................................................... 36 HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 3 Key Terms Learners: students in formal education (secondary, tertiary, post-graduate, or vocational) and professionals in the workforce engaging in learning activities for the purpose of gaining knowledge, skills, and expertise. Learning: the cognitive process whereby information is processed and integrated into retrievable and usable knowledge or skills. Educators: teachers, coaches, mentors, supervisors, instructors, and other roles that aim to enhance and facilitate the learning of learners. Education: the processes and structures within which learning is the primary outcome, including systematic instruction such as in schools, universities, and other courses. HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 4 Background The Landscape of Modern Education Despite its noble intentions, modern education still faces many complex problems. Regardless of the continuous efforts by educators, policy-makers, and researchers, many educators are overworked, and learners are often underserved, especially in areas of more significant resource constraints. However, these issues may be inevitable consequences of purposeful and cautious education systems (driven by research and responsible practice) operating in the context of a rapidly changing society (driven by less inhibited commercialisation). Consequently, the gap between what society demands of its learners and what the education system can provide continues to widen. The repercussions of this gap transcend beyond formal education as many professionals navigate a labour market without optimised learning habits. Moreover, they are expected to engage in continuous self-directed, unstructured learning in evolving fields of knowledge. In the current era of artificial intelligence, learner empowerment is even more crucial as the value of using and adapting knowledge far outstrips the value of access to knowledge. Despite this widening gap, most educational research focuses on pedagogical solutions (how to teach better), with little emphasis on how people can learn better. This situation is problematic as conventional approaches rely almost exclusively on educators (e.g. teachers, mentors, supervisors, coaches, and lecturers) to deliver unrealistically ‘perfect’ teaching. Even if knowledge is delivered with perfect teaching, the learner must still keep up with changes and developments in the field (Chai & Kong, 2017; Engeström, 2015). Learners must be trained to take control and ownership of their own learning and bridge the societal demand/preparation gap themselves. As Kegan (2018) remarks in his commentary on contemporary learning, a transformation in the learner is needed as it is not sustainable or realistic for teachers to address issues with learning through greater “policing”. HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 5 The Black Box of Learning Unfortunately, learners generally lack the knowledge and tools to tackle the challenges of modern learning efficiently. As research on learners’ perspectives is relatively sparse, theories of learning tend to be incomplete, unrealistic, or simply unknown by most (Engeström, 2015). Researchers and educational scholars have noted that even among teacher training, theories of learning are often absent, minimal, or sometimes factually inaccurate (J. A. T. Hattie & Donoghue, 2018; Newton & Salvi, 2020). As a result, there is a black box of learning in which learners are aware that an input of time and effort is required to achieve their desired outcomes, but it is unclear how that input is most effectively transformed into results (see Figure 1). When results are inconsistent or do not seem proportional to the time and effort investment, this can cause anxiety, frustration, demotivation, and changes in self- beliefs over time (Saxena, 2020). These negative consequences can subsequently manifest as unproductive or harmful compensatory behaviour (see Figure 2) (Deb et al., 2015; Lee & Larson, 2000; Zajacova et al., 2005). Figure 1 The black box of learning In addition, popular methods and apps are often misleading. Many “learning solutions” use an oversimplified view of learning or outright ignore decades of research on learning and neuroscience (J. A. T. Hattie & Donoghue, 2018). For example, the usage of lower-order spaced retrieval practice through digital flashcard apps is one of the most common learning strategies relied upon by students (Zung et al., 2022), with dozens of apps claiming this to be a highly effective method. However, research has repeatedly shown that HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 6 flashcards, in the way they are commonly used, are extensively limited in producing consistent performance and learning outcomes (Ariel & Karpicke, 2018; Bjork et al., 2013; Donoghue & Hattie, 2015; Dunlosky et al., 2013; Hays et al., 2010; Karpicke & Roediger, 2008). Figure 2 The black box of learning and its sequelae (consequences) Therefore, learners must be equipped with the knowledge and skills to discern which strategies are productive. To meet the challenges of modern learning, students and professionals must be metacognitive and engage in self-regulated learning (that is, the ability to monitor and adjust their learning strategies to achieve their goals). This need is strongly recognised by both the World Economic Forum and the OECD, which declare self-regulated learning development as a critical necessity and one of the highest priorities for the future of education (OECD, 2019; World Economic Forum, 2023). However, at present, research on the core components for self-regulated learning, including metacognition, cue-utilisation, behavioural science, higher-order learning, learning strategies instruction, and cognitive load theory, are disparate and difficult to operationally distil. Consequently, there is an urgent need for research on learning to be consolidated through a practical perspective for learners to utilise and take ownership of their learning journey. HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 7 Introduction to the HUDLE™ Framework HUDLE™ is iCanStudy’s evidence-based framework for developing effective learning and self-management skills in the modern world. The acronym stands for Holistic Upskilling for Deep processing and Learning Efficiency. The framework provides practical guidelines for developing training strategies and a robust set of theoretical first principles for implementation and facilitation. The approaches are suitable for secondary students, tertiary students, and professional learners and are adapted from a wide range of learning theories and domains that have been tested through rigorous practical application by iCanStudy. The framework draws inspiration from the work of Boekaerts (1997), Dignath et al. (2008), Thiede and Dunlosky (1999), and Hattie and Donoghue (2016). The models for learning and memory are adapted from the classical depiction by Atkinson and Shiffrin (1968) but modified to be more holistic and address the criticisms of typical structural models of memory (Malmberg et al., 2019). Note that specific implementation processes are omitted as they vary depending on the delivery methods used and resource availability of the organisation. iCanStudy currently utilises a hybrid learning environment on a custom online learning experience platform tailored for best-practice cognitive skills development. Holistic Learning never occurs in isolation. When the environment, structures, and variations in an individual’s self-regulating ability are not considered, the resulting model can be problematic or ineffective (Illeris, 2018). Research on the non-academic components of learning is broad, extensive, and often inaccessible to most educators. As a result, it can be overwhelming and confusing for educators to address the holistic needs of learners (Jennings & Greenberg, 2009; Street, 2017). The HUDLE™ framework seeks to simplify the most influential factors under the term enablers of learning to emphasise their relationship to learning outcomes and frame them with an actionable focus. Enablers of learning can be defined as those skills, HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 8 attributes, or conditions which must be met to overcome the common and predictable holistic barriers to effective learning. The high-level interaction between enablers, learning skills, outcomes, and barriers to outcomes is represented in Figure 3. At present, the following enablers are considered crucial and heavily inter-related: • Time management • Task management • Growth mindset • Social learning • Focus management Figure 3 Relationship between enablers of learning, learning skills, barriers, and outcomes Importantly, simplification for easier implementation should not be confused with simplicity in concept. Human psychology, behaviour, social interaction, mindset, personality, and their respective influences on learning are unfathomably complex and often significant. Although this framework distils them into key, practice-centric enablers, practitioners should be sensitive to individual variation and escalate to specialists when appropriate. The full list HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 9 of enablers is outlined in the appendix in Table 1, alongside their respective descriptions, guidelines, and rationales. Upskilling Learning is a composite cognitive skill composed of numerous component skills. The upskilling facet of HUDLE™ relates to the need for targeted and structured skill development. It also aligns with the research on neuroplasticity (Fuchs & Flügge, 2014), opposing the concept that learning ability is a fixed attribute. Upskilling is a core component of HUDLE™ as the skills necessary to process the vast quantities of information for the modern learner are complex, requiring precise methods for training them. First, skill acquisition requires a combination of declarative knowledge (what to do), procedural knowledge (how to do it), and conditional knowledge (when to do it) (Anderson, 1982). To gain this knowledge, both direct teaching and experiential learning are necessary. Second, as learners engage in practice and experience, their ability to monitor their processes and self- correct improves. Finally, these improved skills interact with their learning barriers to provide continuous error feedback, which further improves competency through a cycle of reflective experiential learning (Figure 4). To facilitate reflective practice and the development of learning skills, HUDLE™ utilises a modified Kolb’s experiential learning cycle (D. A. Kolb et al., 1971). The modifications are necessary to address the weaknesses of the original cycle. For example, Kolb’s experiential learning cycle is often associated with Kolb’s learning styles inventory (D. Kolb & Kolb, 2013), which is completely removed in HUDLE™ due to its lack of empirical validity (Cuevas, 2015; Duffin, 2020; Husmann & O’Loughlin, 2019; Khazan, 2018; Newton & Salvi, 2020). Scholars have also criticised Kolb’s experiential cycle for under-acknowledging the impact of social context, emotions, and learner autonomy (T. A. Conklin, 2013; Vince, 1998). The concept of experience as an exclusively past-oriented HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 10 isolated event is also problematic against a compounding, long-term view of self- improvement (Elkjaer, 2008; Miettinen, 2000). Instead, a more cyclical and contextual view of experience is more helpful, as proposed earlier by Dewey (1917) in his practice-centred theory of learning. Despite these criticisms, Kolb’s cycle remains a straightforward framework for promoting reflective practice. Table 2 outlines the HUDLE™ guidelines for utilising a modified Kolb’s cycle that addresses the shortcomings of the original theory. Figure 4 Skill development map HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 11 Notably, all frameworks of reflective practice are dependent on the learner’s level of metacognition. iCanStudy has noticed that the value of reflective experiential learning is markedly moderated by the level of self-awareness and depth of reflection. In Kolb’s cycle, superficial reflections tend to result in inaccurate or illogical abstractions, leading to untargeted and ineffective experiments. This reduces motivation and increases the number of experimentation cycles needed to improve competency. Therefore, additional adjustments are made to encourage deeper, more metacognitive reflections and more accurate abstractions. These are reflected in the guidelines provided in Table 2 of the appendix. As learners progress through multiple compounding experiential cycles, their skill competence improves. However, improvement is limited by consistency and diligence. Learners who cannot persist with practice invariably fail to acquire the skill. Even if the skill has been acquired, the effort and concentration necessary to perform it accurately may be unviable. Thus, skill acquisition can be seen as a process rather than a binary event. The conscious competency model is useful for tracking learners’ competency development through this process (see Adams, n.d.). Strategic utilisation of this model provides valuable metacognitive feedback and positive reinforcement for the learner, improving motivation and self-regulation. In addition, iCanStudy has found that the staged nature of the conscious competence model also provides natural process-based milestones and incremental wins for the learner, which improves growth mindset development. Table 2 includes guidelines for integrating the conscious competence model into skill acquisition facilitation. Deep Processing Deep processing refers to the type of thinking and learning that requires complex cognitive engagement. It involves elaboration, connection with prior knowledge, comparison, and critical evaluation of information. Deep processing is associated with better memory retention and higher levels of knowledge mastery (Craik & Lockhart, 1972). It features integrated, HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 12 networked thinking that is characteristic of higher-order learning, whereas shallow processing is more isolated and corresponds with patterns of lower-order and passive learning (Bloom, 1956; J. Conklin, 2005). The benefits of deep processing are seen in both academic and professional settings with higher engagement, motivation, and performance (Bosch et al., 2021; Chai & Kong, 2017; J. Hattie, 2009; Miterianifa et al., 2021). As common sense may predict, graduates with higher deep processing have also been found to be more employable (Nirmala & Kumar, 2018). However, despite the overwhelming benefits of deep processing, the skills to engage in it are not widely adopted. Although deep processing and its associated higher-order learning strongly correlate with greater expertise and better performance (Hamzah et al., 2022; Miterianifa et al., 2021), it also requires greater mental effort and perceived mental exertion. This mental exertion is termed ‘cognitive load’ (specifically intrinsic or germane cognitive load, in this context) and is beneficial for learning (Ayres & Paas, 2012; Sweller, 2011), often being referred to as desirable difficulty (Bjork & Bjork, 2020; Nihalani & Robinson, 2022). Unfortunately, studies have found that learners tend to avoid desirable difficulty, incorrectly perceiving it as a sign of ineffective learning (Kirk-Johnson et al., 2019). As a result, the learning strategies used by the overwhelming majority of students and professionals tend to be ineffective, passive, and lower-order (Bjork et al., 2013; Donoghue & Hattie, 2015; Groep, 2021; Karpicke et al., 2009; Miyatsu et al., 2018). This avoidance of effortful learning strategies can be mitigated through higher and more accurate metacognition and self-regulated learning instruction (Baars et al., 2020; Blissett et al., 2018; Dunlosky et al., 2020). Lamentably, there is generally sparse guidance on training learners on study strategies in general, let alone deep processing and higher-order learning (McDaniel & Einstein, 2020). Many existing frameworks consider teaching learning skills in isolation, without content. HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 13 However, training learning strategies without context may be realistically impossible (J. A. T. Hattie & Donoghue, 2018). Other frameworks are highly theoretical and are difficult to convert into clear actions for improvement. For example, one of the most comprehensive models of learning is provided by Hattie and Donoghue (2016). In this model, learning is divided into three phases: surface learning (acquiring and consolidating), deep learning (acquiring and consolidating), and transfer of knowledge into new contexts. While it addresses many of the limitations in earlier models, the cognitive processes learners must engage in at each of the three phases overlap. For example, higher-order learning processes are necessary in all three phases. This can be confusing for learners as it describes the phases of learning they should ideally progress through but not the cognitive steps to achieve each phase successfully. In the experience of iCanStudy, a lack of clarity around cognitive processes leads to slower and less accurate metacognitive development, ultimately reducing self-regulation ability. Figure 5 (see Appendix B) further elaborates on the interaction between metacognition, self-regulated learning, higher-order learning, enablers of learning, and overall learning outcomes. HUDLE™ aims to include a more practically-focused approach to deep processing training (see Table 3 of appendix). Based on the observations of iCanStudy’s extensive testing across several thousand adolescent and adult learners, the central suppositions of the HUDLE™ framework’s deep processing aspect (see Table 3) are as follows: 1. Learning systems with insufficient higher-order learning methods are unlikely to be sustainable or effective, especially at high learning volumes or where knowledge must be retrieved in complex ways. 2. Learning is more effective and efficient when higher-order learning strategies are used at the early stages of learning. HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 14 3. Lower-order learning strategies should be used for detailed information that is difficult or time-consuming to integrate via higher-order strategies. 4. The “best” learning strategy is context and stage-dependent and will likely change over time. Therefore, the highest priority is to equip learners with self-regulated learning skills based on strong metacognition. Importantly, deep processing should not be considered the only component necessary for effective learning. Deep processing must be a key component of a learning system, working in conjunction with other evidence-based techniques to address a diverse scope of learning needs. For example, using spaced retrieval alongside interleaving is crucial to prevent knowledge decay (Chen et al., 2021; Ebbinghaus, 1885; Logan et al., 2012). Lower- order learning strategies are also necessary for some assessments which require isolated fact recall. Pragmatically, for any learning strategy to be feasible and sustainable, they must become habits, allowing learners to use them energy-efficiently. Learning Efficiency The learning efficiency aspect of HUDLE™ refers to the underlying priority for real-world results. The modern learner is under constant time constraints. Starting from school, the pressures of high workloads, competitive assessments, and limited time contribute to a substantial global mental health burden on youth (Australian Government Department of Health, 2015; Deb et al., 2015; Lee & Larson, 2000; Zajacova et al., 2005). Thus, learning strategies must help learners achieve their knowledge mastery goals time-efficiently. Unfortunately, as accurate, evidence-based learning strategies instruction is not yet mainstream, many learners rely on strategies that are ineffective or time-consuming, such as lower-order flashcards, passive reading and writing, and passive or active lower-order note- taking (Donoghue & Hattie, 2015; Husmann & O’Loughlin, 2019; Kornell & Bjork, 2008; Miyatsu et al., 2018; Taraban et al., 2000; Zung et al., 2022). Ultimately, a prerequisite to HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 15 efficient learning is that it must produce desirable outcomes in authentic learning environments and pressures, not just in research labs. The emphasis on real-world outcomes is particularly pertinent when considering how learners decide on learning strategies. As discussed previously, learners tend to opt for easier strategies that are unknowingly less effective (Baars et al., 2020; Kirk-Johnson et al., 2019). This is compounded by an additional tendency for learners in academic settings to use “achieving strategies” (Biggs, 1993; Donoghue & Hattie, 2015). Achieving strategies are those strategies where the lowest amount of time and effort is needed to achieve the desired grade. While this may be considered a reasonable decision at first, it can result in mimicry of expertise, whereby learners can superficially appear competent but instead have used surface learning to regurgitate information and fulfil assessment rubrics. In doing so, learners may fail to develop the skills needed for higher-order thinking and deep processing, be unable to overcome more complex challenges, and lack the foundational knowledge for more advanced learning (Bailey et al., 2020; J. Hattie, 2009; Schneider & Bradford, 2020). Anecdotally, similar behaviours are widespread among professional learners also. Learning efficiency, as we describe, has been integrated within the other aspects of HUDLE™ and does not have a separate set of guidelines. Thus, to mitigate unproductive learner tendencies, learning strategies must be able to meet the diverse set of short-term outcomes that learners face, as well as the holistic, long-term goals of transformative and meaningful learning. To achieve these goals, HUDLE™ is designed on a strong, empirical research base to fully equip learners and prepare them for success in the modern world. Memory, 30(8), 923–941. https://doi.org/10.1080/09658211.2022.2058553 HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 30 Appendix A Table 1 Enablers of learning Enabler Description Guidelines Time management Ability to manage and organise tasks and responsibilities in a schedule. • Learners should have a schedule, tracked digitally or physically. • Basic time-blocking and time-boxing principles should be utilised appropriately. Task management Ability to prioritise tasks accurately. • Task prioritisation systems such as Eisenhower’s matrix should be used regularly, and the difference between urgency and importance should be clear. • Task prioritisation should be considered more impactful than time management strategies. Growth mindset Ability to embrace mistakes and experimentation to learn and develop in a mentally healthy way. • Goals should be process-focused or marginal gains in nature. • Tasks should be implemented whereby the condition for successful task completion is mistake identification and reflection. • Effort and adherence to processes should be monitored, celebrated, and evaluated. • Outcomes should be evaluated and discussed only in the context of providing information about the preceding processes. Motivation management Ability to engage in tasks and activities that stimulate intrinsic motivation, while understanding the utility of motivation as a tool. • Behaviours should be evaluated for motivation dependency and shifted towards habit-driven behaviours instead. • Barriers to desirable behaviour should be minimised or removed where possible and environments should be reasonably modified so that desirable behaviours are a natural consequence. • Behavioural change should focus on vulnerability factors, preceding events, antecedents, and reinforcers, with reduced emphasis on changing behaviour through willpower. • Learning processes should be active, engaging, and therefore naturally enjoyable to improve intrinsic motivation. Attempting to improve motivation for an inherently unenjoyable process such as passive learning should be deprioritised. HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 31 Social learning Presence of a supportive environment of peers and leaders to encourage, create discourse, and facilitate a learner identity. • Communities of Practice frameworks should be adhered to where possible, especially with formation of core groups and directing the community to become self-sufficient. • Learning tasks should promote reflection, collaboration, and encouragement among peers. • Communities should be well-moderated but not over-policed. Learners should have freedom and safety to share and connect organically with peers. • Behavioural guidelines and community onboarding should be deliberate and carefully executed to avoid deviant behaviour and interactions. Focus management Ability to manage and regulate attention and concentration to complete desired tasks in a reasonable time to sufficient quality. • Environments should reasonably modified to allow deep work and focus to be more easily achieved. • Learners should keep metacognitive logs to improve focus self-regulation over time. • Quality sleep, diet, and exercise should be encouraged and fundamentals of sustainable focus. • Focus training as an adaptation of mindfulness meditation should be facilitated to improve self-regulation over time. Trauma-informed practice is recommended. Note. Referenced rationalisations of guidelines are available in Appendix B under the respective heading. HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 32 Table 2 Upskilling guidelines Core principle Description Guidelines Types of knowledge Knowledge required for skill acquisition involves declarative, procedural, and conditional knowledge. • Declarative knowledge should always be paired with encouragement to develop procedural and conditional knowledge through experimentation. • Declarative knowledge should be aimed at giving learners the first principles necessary to problem- solve and make meaningful and productive adjustments while they experiment. • Declarative knowledge should be significantly lower in volume and acquisition time investment compared to practice and reflection time. • Practice should always challenge the learner with different perspectives, subjects, content, and types of challenges to promote accurate conditional knowledge. • Progression of skill development should be measured against their competence (as per the conscious competence model) Experiential learning cycles Reflective practice is crucial to develop procedural and conditional knowledge with accuracy and competency. • Kolb’s experiential learning cycle can be used as a basic framework for reflection and targeted experimentation. • Kolb’s learning styles inventory should be entirely removed from consideration and resources. Any resources that feature the learning cycle in conjunction with the learning styles inventory should be omitted or modified. • Experiences should be considered any process for which there can be learning extracted. This is not limited to academic experiences only. Experiences should always be process-oriented rather than outcome-oriented. • Reflection should consider all of the following: the events; emotions during the events and steps (including areas of difficulty); contributing factors to the emotions; specific triggers for difficulty; responses to difficulties; and reactions of others to the individual’s actions (if relevant). • Abstractions should be analytical rather than theoretical. Information from the reflection should be examined for trends, tendencies, biases, and behavioural patterns. • Experiments should be actionable, concise, and limited in number to prevent overload. • Experiments from one cycle should always feed into the experience of the next cycle. • Difficulties or incompetency with later steps should undergo a root cause analysis, noting that deficiencies in prior steps of the cycle directly impact the success of the next. HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 33 • Reflection sessions can be timeboxed for those with low self-awareness or metacognition who struggle with reflective practice until fundamental reflective competency develops. Conscious competence model Learners develop skills in accordance with the conscious competence model. They navigate through the stages of unconscious incompetence, conscious incompetence, conscious competence, and unconscious competence. • The differences in characteristics, anticipated outcomes, challenges, and successes at each stage of competence should be made clear and explicit so that learners are able to assess their skill level and gain positive reinforcement of progress. • Reflective experiential learning cycles should be framed as the method through which competence develops. • Advice, feedback, and decisions to provide additional declarative knowledge should always be catered to the competence level of the learner. Guidance should seek to progress them to the next stage of competence without attempting to skip stages. HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 34 Table 3 Deep processing instruction guidelines Core principle Description Guidelines Metacognition Thinking about thinking. Metacognition refers to the self- awareness of an individual’s own thoughts and cognitive processes. • Reflective experiential practice should be encouraged (see Table 2). • Supervisors, trainers, mentors, and other educators should use metacognitive prompts as a routine part of giving advice and problem-solving. The learner should be encouraged to explore and reflect on their cognitive processes and build a cause-and-effect understanding of their methods and the subsequent outcomes. Self-regulated learning The ability to monitor and adjust learning processes in alignment with learning goals. Self- regulated learning is dependent on accurate metacognition and sufficient declarative knowledge on which adjustments are productive. • Reflective experiential practice should include regular experimentation with learning processes and methods. Experiments should be based on the learner’s developing metacognition and declarative knowledge of higher-order learning principles and techniques. • Learners should be instructed to be vigilant of unproductive adjustments, such as aversion to desirable cognitive load. Higher-order learning Integrated thinking that involves comparison, judgement, and prioritisation of information. Higher-order learning is associated with deeper and more meaningful knowledge, better memory retention, and more complex knowledge retrieval. • Learners should be instructed about the explicit features and principles of higher-order learning vs lower-order learning, as well as the respective costs and benefits. • The adjustments made as part of self-regulated learning should aim to increase higher-order learning processes at earlier stages of the learning process. • Learners should be taught about the high mental effort required with higher-order learning and the beneficial effects of this effort. Additionally, learners should be encouraged not to avoid cognitive load and revert to prior lower-order habits. • Non-linear note-taking should be taught and role-modelled to facilitate higher-order learning and deep processing. Linear note-taking and verbatim note-taking should be generally discouraged unless used for creating future reference documentation (Bravo Palacios & Simons, 2021; Bretzing & Kulhavy, 1979; Jansen et al., 2017; Voyer et al., 2022). HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 35 Appendix B Figure 5 Elaborated overview of learning skills, enablers, barriers, and learning outcomes HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 36 Rationales for Enablers of Learning Guidelines As each concept is complex and dense, the rationales in this appendix are not a comprehensive overview of every factor. It serves as a brief outline of some of the core principles and research findings supporting the guidelines. A substantial body of research and nuance is omitted for conciseness. Time Management Several books in the productivity space have discussed the benefit of time-blocking and time- boxing (Morgenstern, 2004; Newport, 2016; Oakley et al., 2018). Although there is less academic research, several studies have found similar benefits for time management strategies with principles similar to time-blocking (Britton & Tesser, 1991; Claessens et al., 2007). Similar beneficial effects have been found in moderation in iCanStudy’s practice. Importantly, the concept of ‘discounted utility’ suggests that the ability of people to use their time effectively is limited by their perception of task priority rather than fundamental scheduling practices (König & Kleinmann, 2007). Task Management High-quality task management allows for productive and effective usage of time. As such, task prioritisation allows time management to result in net productive output. There is generally sparse academic research on task prioritisation and management compared to the popularity of discussion in non-academic books. Some studies have found the effects to be beneficial and significant (Claessens et al., 2007; Monsalve et al., 2020), with the benefit improving with better structure and guidance (Bratterud et al., 2020). These beneficial effects have been consistently seen with high significance in iCanStudy’s practice. Growth Mindset Dweck (2012) established the importance of a growth mindset in overcoming adversity. However, there are various nuances to developing growth mindset that are often omitted. HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 37 Firstly, growth mindset is likely to be situational rather than a constant state of mind embodied by certain individuals at all times (Dweck, 2012; Haimovitz & Dweck, 2017). Secondly, it has been well demonstrated that improving growth mindset through interventions is challenging (Credé et al., 2017; J. A. T. Hattie & Donoghue, 2018; Schwartz et al., 2016; Yeager et al., 2016). In iCanStudy’s observation and practice, tasks and activities must be precisely to avoid psychological reactance (Steindl et al., 2015) and circumvent learned helplessness (Alloy & Abramson, 1982; Duckworth & Seligman, 2005; Seligman, 1974). iCanStudy’s practice has also observed that cognitive dissonance can occur when growth mindset is taught declaratively without the enablement of cognitive and emotional skills to act on it. In these instances, the individual can become more self-critical and anxious as they now feel they are failing themselves by being unable to engage in growth opportunities. Thus, it is crucial to equip learners with practical skills and realistic steps towards developing a growth mindset. Arguably, as research on behaviour change and identity psychology has shown a reciprocal relationship between cognition (belief and thoughts) and behaviour (actions) (Bandura, 1986), it may be more important to give learners actionable steps than a conceptual understanding of growth mindsets. Motivation Management Motivation is complex and multifactorial. Motivation overall seems to be strongly related to the individual’s sense of identity and beliefs (Higgins, 1997), and intrinsic motivation is largely derived from an individual’s experience of a task (Deci et al., 1981). Therefore, many popular strategies relying on extrinsic motivators, such as motivational videos, tend to be short-lived, unsustainable, or even harmful (Deci et al., 1999). Strategies that increase intrinsic motivation through finding greater enjoyment and engagement with the process itself boost performance more consistently and are associated with better learning outcomes (Cerasoli et al., 2014; Ryan & Deci, 2000). Studies have strongly shown that passive learning HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 38 is inherently unmotivating and disengaging, while active learning achieves the opposite (Mahmood et al., 2011; Pi et al., 2022; Saxena, 2020; Schnaubert & Schneider, 2022). This is enhanced when concurrently reinforced by real-world success (J. A. Hattie & Donoghue, 2016). Relying on motivation for behaviours is also detrimental to long-term success. Research on behavioural science has shown that antecedents, consequences, and environment are not only more dominant drivers of new habit formation and behaviour but are also more sustainable (Davis et al., 2015; Kwasnicka et al., 2016; Michie & Johnston, 2012). Over- reliance on willpower daily may also cause willpower depletion and burnout, although this relationship is still being investigated (Baumeister, 2014; Job et al., 2010). Social Learning As per the critical theory perspective on learning, the social context within which a learner operates is fundamental and directive to the type of learning they engage in (see Brookfield, 2004). Attempting to address every social variable is unrealistically complex for a single practitioner or educator. Therefore, the HUDLE™ approach develops the individual’s academic buoyancy and self-regulated learning skills to navigate their social context more easily with reduced impact of obstacles. Self-regulated learning skills have been shown to mitigate against the effects of a diverse range of extrinsic barriers (Bjork et al., 2013; Hadwin et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2022). Theories around situated cognition also tell us that our ability to learn is strongly influenced by our community and exposure (see Kim & Merriam, 2010). One of the most popular and acclaimed approaches for developing effective communities is the Communities of Practice (CoP) framework, which can be considered a direct offshoot of situated cognition theory (see Wenger, 2011). iCanStudy has evaluated the efficacy of the CoP framework through the creation of an international online community of over 20,000 members. It was HUDLE™ Framework Copyright © 2023, iCanStudy Pty Limited 39 found that the CoP framework is suitable for online applications at scale, although carefully designed behavioural guidelines and onboarding are critical to account for diversity in cultural perceptions, norms, and online communication etiquette (see Preece & Maloney- Krichmar, 2005). Focus Management Focus responds poorly to intention and willpower. Instead, focus appears to be a by-product of appropriate fundamental conditions. Sleep, diet, and exercise are well-established enablers of optimum cognitive performance and focus (Bono & Hill, 2022; Gómez-Pinilla, 2008; Hillman et al., 2008). Similarly, creating an optimal environment for focus is more emphasised in modern advice than intention-based strategies (Abdukadirov, 2016; Arno & Thomas, 2016; Clear, 2018; Newport, 2016). To make the appropriate adjustments to their environments, learners must engage in self-regulation, for which logging can improve by enhancing metacognition and self-awareness (Bjork et al., 2013; Chick, 2019; Saxena, 2020; Zimmerman & Schunk, 2011). One of the few cognitive strategies that have been shown to improve focus is mindfulness meditation (Chiesa & Serretti, 2010), for which there are several variations. Notably, the benefits of mindfulness meditation are more significant in the far-transfer of self-regulation skills than the immediate benefits (Tang et al., 2015).
